baseline_kernel:
// f32 GEMM - full precision but 2x memory bandwidth
// bf16 has same range as f32 (8-bit exponent) but lower precision (7-bit mantissa)
analysis: bf16 exponent matches f32 (8 bits) - same dynamic range, no overflow issues
bf16 mantissa is 7 bits vs f32's 23 - sufficient for ML gradients
2x bandwidth efficiency: 2 bytes vs 4 bytes per element
schedule_reasoning: bf16 tensor cores same throughput as f16 on Ampere
256x128 wide tile for large M dimension
4 stages for deep pipelining given large problem size
Native bf16 MMA: no conversion overhead unlike f16→bf16

constraints.special: Attention score computation - bf16 in, f32 out for softmax

baseline_kernel:
// bf16 throughout causes softmax numerical issues
// Need f32 output for stable softmax normalization
analysis: Attention scores can be large before softmax - bf16 output risks overflow
Softmax denominator sum needs f32 precision
bf16 input fine - Q, K matrices are normalized
schedule_reasoning: bf16 MMA with f32 accumulator - natural tensor core mode
f32 output written directly, no conversion
128x256 tile matches attention shape (seq_len × seq_len)
Used in: transformer attention, BERT, GPT
concept: BF16 attention - f32 output for softmax stability

constraints.special: Drop-in f32 replacement - same API, 8x faster

baseline_kernel:
// Standard f32 GEMM using FMA units
// No tensor core utilization - scalar operations only
analysis: TF32 truncates f32 mantissa from 23 to 10 bits during MMA
Range preserved (8-bit exponent), precision reduced
Automatic in cuBLAS - users don't see the truncation
schedule_reasoning: TF32 uses 16x8x8 MMA - same as f64 instruction shape
f32 data loaded, internally truncated to TF32 for MMA
8x faster than FMA-only f32 GEMM
Transparent acceleration: no code changes for existing f32 workloads
concept: TF32 transparent acceleration - f32 API with tensor cores

constraints.special: Large K reduction - precision impact assessment

baseline_kernel:
// Pure f32 accumulation for K=8192
// TF32 truncation happens per MMA, errors may accumulate
analysis: TF32 truncates each operand - K=8192 means 8192 truncated products summed
Error is typically < 0.1% for ML workloads
For HPC requiring full f32: use CUDA math mode to disable TF32
schedule_reasoning: Large K benefits from split-K to increase parallelism
5 stages for deep K pipelining
Split-K=4 partitions K=8192 into 4 chunks of 2048
TF32 error bounded by sqrt(K) in worst case - still acceptable
concept: TF32 large-K GEMM - precision vs performance tradeoff

constraints.special: Backward pass - data gradient for 3D CNN

baseline_kernel:
// Naive dgrad: transpose filter and run fprop
// But 3D filter transpose is complex: KTRSC → CTRSK
analysis: Dgrad computes gradient w.r.t. input given output gradient
Mathematically: dX = conv(dY, rotate180(W))
3D makes rotation more complex: flip T, R, S dimensions
schedule_reasoning: CUTLASS dgrad kernel handles filter transformation internally
Optimized iterator precomputes rotated offsets
Same tile shape as fprop - memory patterns similar
Critical for 3D video understanding: C3D, I3D, SlowFast
concept: Conv3d Dgrad - 3D data gradient for video backprop

baseline_kernel:
// Naive wgrad: correlate input with output gradient
// dW[k,t,r,s,c] = sum over n,d,h,w of X[n,d+t,h+r,w+s,c] * dY[n,d,h,w,k]
analysis: Wgrad reduces over N,D,H,W - massive parallelism but reduction bottleneck
Each output weight element accumulates N*D*H*W products
Different from fprop/dgrad: output is small (filter), input is large (activations)
schedule_reasoning: Wgrad maps to implicit GEMM with transposed roles
Split-K over spatial dims to parallelize reduction
Smaller tile (128x64) because output (weights) is small
bf16 for training stability with f32 accumulator
concept: Conv3d Wgrad - weight gradient with spatial reduction

constraints.special: Upper triangular, non-unit diagonal, left side

baseline_kernel:
// D = A * B where A is upper triangular (left side)
// Naive: check every element if above/below diagonal
analysis: Left side TRMM: D = A * B, A is triangular
Upper triangular: A[i,j] = 0 for i > j
Non-unit diagonal: explicit diagonal values (not assumed 1)
schedule_reasoning: SideMode::kLeft for D = A * B (A on left)
FillMode::kUpper skips tiles entirely below diagonal
DiagType::kNonUnit loads actual diagonal values
Tile scheduler computes only upper triangular region of A
concept: TRMM left upper - triangular multiply for Cholesky solve

baseline_kernel:
// Complex TRMM: D = A * B where A is complex triangular
// Complex multiply: (a+bi)(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i
analysis: Complex triangular common in: quantum mechanics, signal processing
Triangular structure still applies: skip elements above/below diagonal
Complex MMA requires 4 real MMA operations internally
schedule_reasoning: Smaller tile for complex: 2x register pressure vs real
64x64 tile with 16-deep K
Complex TRMM schedules 4 real sub-GEMMs: RR, RI, IR, II
Used in: Hermitian eigensolvers, QR factorization
concept: Complex TRMM - triangular multiply for quantum/signal

constraints.special: Lower fill, transpose A: C = A^T * A

baseline_kernel:
// C = A^T * A where C is symmetric
// Naive: compute full N×N output, discard upper triangle
analysis: A^T * A pattern common in: normal equations, Gram matrix
Result is symmetric by construction: (A^T*A)^T = A^T*A
Only need to compute and store lower (or upper) triangle
schedule_reasoning: SYRK with A transposed: effective GEMM is A^T (K×N) × A (K×N)^T
FillMode::kLower computes only lower triangle tiles
50% compute reduction vs full GEMM
Common in: least squares, covariance matrices
concept: SYRK A^T*A - Gram matrix / normal equations

baseline_kernel:
// f64 SYRK: C = A * A^T, C symmetric
// HPC requires full double precision - no TF32 truncation
analysis: f64 SYRK for: scientific computing, financial risk models
SM80 DMMA: 8x8x4 f64 tensor core instruction
8 bytes per element - memory bound for large problems
schedule_reasoning: f64 tensor cores (DMMA) available on Ampere
64x64 tile due to 4x register pressure vs f16
8x8x4 DMMA instruction shape
Used in: climate models, quantum chemistry, portfolio optimization
concept: SYRK f64 - double precision covariance for HPC

analysis: SYR2K: symmetric rank-2k update
Result is symmetric: (A*B^T + B*A^T)^T = B*A^T + A*B^T = same
Fusing both terms eliminates temporary and halves A,B loads
schedule_reasoning: SYR2K fuses A*B^T and B*A^T into single kernel
Tile scheduler only computes lower triangle (symmetry)
A and B each loaded once, used for both terms
Used in: eigenvalue problems, model updates

constraints.special: Double precision SYR2K for scientific computing

baseline_kernel:
// f64 SYR2K - needs full precision accumulation
// DMMA tensor cores for 8x speedup over FMA
analysis: f64 SYR2K for: structural mechanics, fluid dynamics
DMMA provides tensor core acceleration for f64
8 bytes/element limits arithmetic intensity
schedule_reasoning: 64x64 tile appropriate for f64 register pressure
DMMA 8x8x4 instruction
Upper fill - compute only upper triangle
Fortran-style ColMajor for LAPACK compatibility
concept: SYR2K f64 - double precision for structural mechanics

constraints.special: Hermitian rank-2k: C = alpha*A*B^H + conj(alpha)*B*A^H + beta*C

baseline_kernel:
// Complex HER2K - Hermitian version of SYR2K
// B^H = conjugate transpose, not just transpose
analysis: HER2K: Hermitian rank-2k, result is Hermitian (C^H = C)
Conjugate transpose B^H means conj(B)^T
Diagonal of result is real (imaginary parts cancel)
schedule_reasoning: HER2K uses ComplexTransform::kConjugate for B^H
Result is Hermitian - diagonal forced real in epilogue
64x64 for complex (2x register pressure)
Used in: quantum mechanics, antenna array processing
concept: HER2K - Hermitian rank-2k for quantum computing
